instruction: Explain lvalues, rvalues, and move semantics.

input.title: Move Semantics

output.technical: An lvalue is an object with an identifiable memory address. An rvalue is a temporary value. `std::move` casts an lvalue to an rvalue reference (`&&`), enabling the compiler to 'steal' its resources rather than copy them.
output.beginner: Normally, giving data to a function makes a copy. 'Move semantics' lets you say 'I'm done with this variable, you can just take it', which is incredibly fast because it skips copying.
output.analogy: Copying is like reading a document and writing an exact copy on a new sheet of paper. Moving is just handing the original piece of paper to someone else.
output.solution: Use `std::move` to transfer ownership of heavy objects (like vectors and strings) when the original variable is no longer needed.
output.prevention: Do not access an object after it has been moved from, as its state is valid but unspecified (usually empty).

output.solution: Use `std::move` to transfer ownership of heavy objects (like vectors and strings) when the original variable is no longer needed.
output.prevention: Do not access an object after it has been moved from, as its state is valid but unspecified (usually empty).

instruction: Explain lvalues, rvalues, and move semantics.

input.title: Move Semantics

output.technical: An lvalue is an object with an identifiable memory address. An rvalue is a temporary value. `std::move` casts an lvalue to an rvalue reference (`&&`), enabling the compiler to 'steal' its resources rather than copy them.
output.beginner: Normally, giving data to a function makes a copy. 'Move semantics' lets you say 'I'm done with this variable, you can just take it', which is incredibly fast because it skips copying.
output.analogy: Copying is like reading a document and writing an exact copy on a new sheet of paper. Moving is just handing the original piece of paper to someone else.
output.solution: Use `std::move` to transfer ownership of heavy objects (like vectors and strings) when the original variable is no longer needed.
output.prevention: Do not access an object after it has been moved from, as its state is valid but unspecified (usually empty).
